text: Both fallopian tubes are visibly adhered to the vaginal cuff. The right fallopian tube is identified as problematic, while the left tube shows a hydrosalpinx and appears swollen. The surgeon initiates a retroperitoneal approach lateral to the vaginal cuff, carefully avoiding the pelvic sidewall vessels and ureter.

The surgeon performs tedious blunt and sharp dissection to separate the tubal remnant and hydrosalpinx from the pelvic sidewall and left ovary. A small follicular cyst is fulgurated. The left ovary is preserved due to its relatively healthy condition, while the fallopian tube adhered to the vaginal cuff is removed.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and performs a retroperitoneal approach around the infundibulopelvic vessels. The infundibulopelvic ligament and ovarian vessels are clamped, fulgurated, and cut, ensuring the right ureter is not compromised.

The surgeon begins a retroperitoneal dissection to separate the right ovarian and tubal abscess from the pelvic sidewall, ensuring the ureter is safely checked. The bladder flap is carefully detached from the vaginal cuff, revealing the fallopian tube's intimate involvement with the bladder muscularis. Tedious dissection is performed to safely separate these structures.

The surgeon performs a vaginotomy to identify the tubal remnant within the vagina. An endoanal sizer is used to manipulate the vaginal tissue, locating the entry point for the vaginotomy. Traction and counter traction are applied to free the fallopian tube, which is visibly adhered to the vaginal cuff.

The surgeon carefully teases the fallopian tube off the vaginal cuff and angle, releasing abscess fluid from the tube. The remaining portions of the fallopian tube are removed and delivered through an endocatch bag. Hemostasis is ensured, and the vaginal cuff is closed using a figure-of-eight imbricating vicryl suture.

The surgeon completes a modified male McCall coloplasty to address a wide cul-de-sac and prevent vaginal cuff prolapse. A piece of adhesion barrier is placed over the vaginal cuff and moistened with saline to prevent adhesion formation.
